title: Marksmanship, Firearm Types & the Use-of-Force Continuum
pillar_name: Physical Literacy, Martial Arts & Human Performance
sub_domain_name: Firearms, Defensive Tools & the Use-of-Force Continuum

required.title: [Equipment] .22 LR handgun (e.g., Ruger Mark IV, Smith & Wesson SW22 Victory)

suggested.title: [Equipment] Cleaning kit appropriate to calibers trained

competence_markers.description: The student safely handles, loads, fires, clears, and cleans at least two firearm types
The student demonstrates consistent marksmanship — tight groups at appropriate distances for each platform
The student can articulate the full use-of-force continuum and identify the appropriate response level for various scenarios
The student can de-escalate a simulated confrontation verbally
The student understands and can explain the basic legal framework of defensive force
The student demonstrates safe storage awareness — understands the obligation and can explain why it matters
Four Rules compliance is habitual and consistent across all handling situations

cross_connections.description: Empty-hand capability is the step before armed defense on the continuum. A person who can grapple has options.
Situational awareness is the perceptual layer firearms proficiency sits on.
Self-defense law connects to the broader study of legal systems.
The discipline of safe handling and the judgment of proportional force are character tests.

content.why_this_matters:
You know the Four Rules. They are reflexive. Now you learn to shoot — with real firearms, with real ammunition, under real supervision. You also learn the full use-of-force continuum: the complete spectrum of responses to threats, from awareness and avoidance at one end to lethal force at the other. This continuum is the organizing framework for everything in 2.1.6, and it teaches the most important lesson about defensive tools: that the best outcome is the one where you never need to use them, and the gun is the last tool on the continuum, not the first.

Marksmanship is the precision skill that makes a firearm useful rather than dangerous. A person who can consistently put rounds where they intend them is a person who can be trusted with the responsibility of armed capability. A person who cannot shoot accurately is a liability — to themselves and to everyone around them. Accuracy comes from fundamentals: sight alignment, trigger press, breathing, and follow-through. These are taught slowly, practiced methodically, and measured objectively. Speed comes later. Precision comes first.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Marksmanship fundamentals** — sight alignment, trigger press, breathing, natural point of aim
2. **Firearm types and function** — rifles, shotguns, handguns; how each works mechanically
3. **Cleaning and maintenance** — field-stripping, cleaning, and reassembling firearms
4. **Storage and security** — the non-negotiable responsibility of safe storage
5. **The complete use-of-force continuum** — from awareness through de-escalation to lethal force
6. **De-escalation practice** — verbal skills for resolving confrontations without physical force
7. **Legal framework basics** — self-defense law, proportionality, and the consequences of force use
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Marksmanship Fundamentals

Marksmanship is the ability to put a bullet exactly where you want it. It is a precision skill built on a few fundamentals practiced thousands of times.

**Sight alignment:** The front sight and rear sight must be perfectly aligned — the front sight centered in the rear sight notch (for iron sights), with equal light on both sides. Focus your eye on the front sight, not the target. The target will be slightly blurry. The front sight must be sharp.

**Sight picture:** Once your sights are aligned, place the front sight on the point of the target where you want the bullet to strike. Hold this picture steady while you press the trigger.

**Trigger press:** This is the most important fundamental. A smooth, steady rearward press on the trigger, straight back, without disturbing your sight picture. Do not jerk, slap, or squeeze the trigger. Press it smoothly until the gun fires. The shot should surprise you slightly — if you know the exact instant the gun will fire, you will flinch, and flinching destroys accuracy.

**Breathing:** Breathe naturally. Take a breath, let it out halfway, and hold. Press the trigger during this natural respiratory pause. Your body is most stable between breaths.

**Natural point of aim:** The direction your body naturally points when relaxed in your shooting position. If your sights drift off target when you relax, your natural point of aim does not match your target. Adjust your body position — not your arms — until your natural point of aim aligns with the target. Fighting your natural point of aim with muscular effort produces fatigue and inconsistency.

**Follow-through:** After the shot breaks, maintain your sight picture and trigger position. Do not immediately drop the rifle or look to see where the shot went. Hold the position for a full second. Follow-through prevents the common error of jerking the gun as the shot fires.

**Practice:** Start at 25 yards with a .22 rifle from a supported position (prone with a rest or bench rest). Focus entirely on fundamentals, not speed. A tight group (all shots within a 2-inch circle) at 25 yards demonstrates mastery of the fundamentals. Then extend to 50 yards, then 100 yards. The same fundamentals apply — they just become more demanding with distance.

### Part 2: Firearm Types and Function

**Rifles:** Long guns with rifled barrels (spiral grooves inside the barrel that spin the bullet for accuracy). Designed for accuracy at distance. Types include bolt-action (manual cycling between shots — the most accurate and simplest), semi-automatic (fires one round per trigger press, automatically loads the next), and lever-action (manual cycling via a lever).

**Shotguns:** Long guns that typically fire multiple projectiles (shot) or a single large projectile (slug). Types include pump-action (manual cycling via a sliding foreend), semi-automatic, and break-action (barrel hinges open to load). Shotguns are versatile: bird shot for small game, buckshot for defense, slugs for large game or longer-range defense.

**Handguns:** Short guns designed for one-handed use (though two-handed shooting is the standard for accuracy). Types include revolvers (a rotating cylinder holds rounds) and semi-automatic pistols (a spring-loaded magazine in the grip feeds rounds). Handguns are portable and concealable, making them the standard defensive firearm — but they are significantly harder to shoot accurately than rifles.

**Ammunition basics:** A cartridge contains four components: the bullet (projectile), the powder (propellant), the case (container), and the primer (ignition source). Caliber refers to the diameter of the bullet and determines the cartridge type. .22 LR, 9mm, .45 ACP, .223/5.56, 12 gauge — each is a specific combination of bullet diameter, case size, and powder charge designed for specific applications.

### Part 3: Cleaning and Maintenance

A firearm is a machine. Machines require maintenance. A neglected firearm is an unsafe firearm — fouling in the barrel affects accuracy, built-up carbon in the action affects reliability, and rust destroys components.

**Field-stripping:** Disassembling the firearm into its major components for cleaning. Every firearm has a field-strip procedure. Learn the procedure for each firearm you train with and be able to execute it confidently.

**Cleaning procedure:** Run a bore brush through the barrel to loosen fouling. Follow with solvent-soaked patches until they come through clean. Apply a light coat of oil to the bore. Clean carbon buildup from the bolt, slide, or action components. Apply oil to moving parts per the manufacturer's recommendation. Reassemble.

**The discipline:** Clean your firearms after every range session. Not sometimes. Every time. This is IC2 discipline applied to equipment maintenance.

### Part 4: Storage and Security

Safe storage is a non-negotiable responsibility. An unsecured firearm is a liability, not an asset.

**Standards:**
- Firearms stored in a locked safe, lock box, or gun cabinet when not in active use
- Ammunition stored separately for additional safety where children are present
- Only authorized users have the combination or key
- The student understands that safe storage is not optional — it is a legal and moral responsibility

**The principle:** You are responsible for every firearm in your possession. If an unauthorized person gains access to your firearm and someone is injured, you bear moral responsibility (and often legal liability). Safe storage prevents this entirely.

### Part 5: The Use-of-Force Continuum

Every encounter that could involve force exists on a spectrum. The continuum, from lowest to highest force level:

**1. Awareness and avoidance (2.1.5):** The best defensive encounter is the one that never happens. Read your environment. Avoid dangerous areas. Maintain situational awareness. Most threats are avoided by simply not being there.

**2. Verbal de-escalation:** Calm, clear communication. Speaking in a low, steady voice. Not escalating with insults, challenges, or threats. Acknowledging the other person's position without conceding yours. "I don't want trouble. I'm leaving." Most potentially violent encounters can be resolved here.

**3. Disengagement:** Leaving. Creating distance. Walking away, driving away, getting behind a locked door. This is not cowardice — it is tactical intelligence. The person who retreats to a safe position has won the encounter without risk.

**4. Empty-hand control (2.1.3):** If disengagement fails and physical contact occurs, your martial arts training provides the ability to control the situation without weapons. Clinch, takedown, pin, hold until the threat stops or help arrives.

**5. Intermediate tools:** OC spray (pepper spray) bridges the gap between empty hands and lethal force. It is effective, legal in most jurisdictions, and non-lethal. Understanding its effects (temporary blindness, respiratory distress, intense pain) and its limitations (wind-affected, not always effective against determined aggressors or people under the influence) makes it a practical defensive option.

**6. Lethal force:** Used only when there is an imminent threat of death or serious bodily harm and no other option is available. Drawing a weapon changes everything — legally, psychologically, and practically. Lethal force is the last resort on the continuum, not an early option.

**The principle:** Most situations that untrained people handle with weapons could have been resolved — more safely, more legally, and more ethically — at an earlier point on the continuum. Every step on the continuum represents an escalation with increasing consequences. The competent person uses the minimum force necessary and de-escalates as soon as possible.

### Part 6: Legal Framework Basics

The law governs when and how you can use force. Understanding this framework is not optional — it is a prerequisite for responsible capability.

**Reasonable fear:** You must have a reasonable belief that you face imminent threat of serious harm.

**Proportionality:** Your response must match the threat. A verbal insult does not justify physical force. A punch does not justify a bullet.

**Imminence:** The threat must be happening now — not last week, not "maybe later."

**The consequences of force:** Even justified force typically results in police investigation, potential arrest, legal proceedings, financial cost, and psychological consequences. The legal system investigates force — it does not congratulate you for it.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Marksmanship
**Developing well:** Consistent groups at target distances. The student can articulate the fundamentals and self-diagnose errors (flinching, trigger jerk, breathing disruption).

**Developing slowly:** Shots scattered widely. The student cannot identify what is going wrong.

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student getting enough range time? Marksmanship improves with volume — hundreds of rounds, not dozens. Is the .22 LR appropriate, or does the student need more air rifle time before progressing?

### 2. Use-of-Force Continuum Application
**Developing well:** The student correctly identifies the appropriate force level for various scenarios. They default to lower levels and escalate only when the scenario demands it.

**Developing slowly:** The student jumps to lethal force in scenarios that could be resolved with de-escalation or disengagement.

**Diagnostic questions:** Are you presenting enough scenarios at lower levels of the continuum? If all your scenarios involve lethal threats, the student will train toward lethal responses. Most scenarios should be resolvable without force.

### 3. Safe Handling Under All Conditions
**Developing well:** Four Rules compliance is habitual across all handling situations — range, cleaning, storage, transitions. The student self-corrects before you correct them.

**Developing slowly:** Occasional muzzle or trigger discipline lapses, especially during transitions (picking up, putting down, moving between positions).

**Diagnostic questions:** Is the student fatigued? Long range sessions degrade safety discipline. Keep sessions under 90 minutes. Are you correcting every violation immediately?
